one_line_takeaway: T-MAP shows that feeding an evolutionary (MAP-Elites) red-teaming search actual multi-step tool-execution feedback -- via LLM-generated success/failure diagnostics and a learned tool-transition graph -- rather than text-only signal roughly doubles the rate at which adversarial prompts against MCP-integrated LLM agents translate into fully realized harmful tool-execution outcomes (57.8% average ARR vs. 32.5% for the strongest text-only evolutionary baseline).
key_finding: T-MAP achieves the highest attack realization rate (ARR) in every one of the five MCP environments, with an average ARR of 57.8% versus SE 32.5%, IR 15.6%, MT 10.0%, ZS 1.9% (Table 1). Per-environment ARR for T-MAP: CodeExecutor 56.2%, Slack 64.1%, Gmail 46.9%, Playwright 37.5%, Filesystem 84.4%; corresponding refusal rates (RR) are 14.1%, 15.6%, 9.4%, 21.9%, 1.6% (average RR 12.5%, lowest among all methods). Diversity: T-MAP finds the most distinct successful tool trajectories (|HL3|=21.80 vs SE 12.80, IR 9.20, MT 6.00, ZS 1.20) while also having the lowest Self-BLEU (0.25 vs SE's 0.45) and lowest embedding cosine similarity (0.47 vs 0.52 for all baselines) -- i.e., SE's higher raw success rate among baselines comes with much lower diversity (Table 2). Judge reliability: DeepSeek-V3.2 judge correlates with human labels at Spearman 0.831 / Pearson 0.830, and with GPT-5.2 at 0.938/0.940, with Opus 4.6 at 0.892/0.891, with Qwen3.5-397B at 0.969/0.968 (Table 3); the judge is somewhat conservative, mislabeling 29.8% of human-L3 cases as L2. Generalization: T-MAP attains the highest ARR against all nine tested frontier models, with Claude Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 showing comparatively higher residual refusal rates (more robust), while Gemini-3-Flash, Kimi-K2.5, and GLM-5 show substantially higher ARR (more vulnerable). Ablation (Table 4, averaged over 5 environments): removing the TCG drops L3 from 58.40% to 45.71% and raises L1 (errors) from 10.95% to 20.13%; removing Cross-Diagnosis raises L0 (refusal) from 11.93% to 15.63%; |HL3| drops from 23.88 (full T-MAP) to 21.38 (w/o TCG) and 21.13 (w/o Cross-Diagnosis). Multi-MCP chains: T-MAP retains the highest ARR and lowest RR across all three multi-server configurations, and achieves a 46.28% cross-server trajectory ratio versus 14.29%-22.97% for baselines (Table 5).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is T-MAP (Trajectory-aware MAP-Elites), an automated red-teaming algorithm for MCP-integrated LLM agents. It maintains an 8x8 evolutionary archive spanning 8 risk categories x 8 attack styles, and on each iteration runs a four-step cycle: (1) Cross-Diagnosis, where an LLMAnalyst extracts success factors from a high-performing 'parent' cell's execution trajectory and failure causes from a 'target' cell's trajectory; (2) trajectory-guided mutation by an LLMMutator that uses both the Cross-Diagnosis output and a learned Tool Call Graph (TCG) -- a directed graph over tools where each edge (ti,tj) stores success/failure counts and rationales for that tool-to-tool transition -- to produce a new candidate attack prompt; (3) execution of the new prompt against the target agent and online update of the TCG with the resulting edge-level outcomes; (4) an LLMJudge that scores the resulting trajectory into one of four discrete success levels and updates the archive if the new prompt is an improvement for its cell. The explicit novelty claimed over prior evolutionary red-teaming (e.g., Rainbow Teaming/Samvelyan et al. 2024, which the paper treats as related work and reimplements as the 'Standard Evolution' baseline) is that T-MAP conditions mutation on actual multi-step tool-execution feedback (via Cross-Diagnosis and the TCG) rather than mutating based on text output alone.
threat_model: Attacker goal: craft natural-language prompts that cause an MCP-integrated LLM agent to not merely emit harmful text but to actually execute a sequence of tool calls that realizes a harmful objective in an external environment (e.g., exfiltrate data, send phishing emails, execute malware, contaminate files). Attack surface: the agent's tool-calling loop across five MCP servers -- CodeExecutor, Slack, Gmail, Playwright, and Filesystem -- and later, multi-server chains combining these. Attacker capability: black-box access to the target agent (only prompts and observed trajectories/tool outputs are used; no gradient or weight access), with an auxiliary attacker LLM (DeepSeek-V3.2) serving as LLMMutator/LLMAnalyst/LLMJudge/LLMTCG. Success is graded on a 4-level scale per trajectory: L0 Refused (no tool calls), L1 Error (tool calls issued but fail due to invalid parameters/permissions/exceptions), L2 Weak Success (partial harmful completion), L3 Realized (harmful intent fully executed through tool calls). The paper explicitly scopes this as a sandboxed-evaluation threat model and states in its own Limitations section that real deployments would add permission checks, user confirmation, input validation, and execution sandboxing that could reduce the reported attack realization rate (ARR) in practice.
limitations: Limitations stated by the authors themselves: (1) all experiments are conducted in sandboxed environments; real-world deployments typically add permission checks, user confirmation, input validation, and execution sandboxing that may prevent the reported ARR from translating directly to practice; (2) the framework relies on DeepSeek-V3.2 as the attacker model, and its relatively weak safety alignment is stated by the authors to contribute to effective adversarial prompt generation -- implying the reported attack strength is partly an artifact of using a weakly-aligned attacker LLM, and that as target-model (and potentially attacker-model) safety alignment improves, framework effectiveness may shift. Additional caveats visible in the text but not framed as formal limitations: the LLM-as-judge, while validated against human annotators, is measurably more conservative on high-severity (L3) cases (29.8% human-L3 mislabeled as L2), so true ARR could be modestly undercounted or, depending on interpretation, the reported figures could be a lower bound on realized harm; risk categories and attack styles are themselves derived from prior benchmarks (Zhang et al. 2025c and Wei et al. 2023) rather than newly validated taxonomies.

datasets_used: Five custom MCP server environments built by the authors: CodeExecutor, Slack, Gmail, Playwright, Filesystem
8x8 archive of 8 risk categories x 8 attack styles (risk categories derived from Zhang et al. 2025c 'Agent-SafetyBench'; attack styles derived from Wei et al. 2023 'Jailbroken')

96 curated attack-prompt/trajectory samples (uniformly sampled across success levels) used for judge-reliability validation, each rated by 4 human annotators (graduate students with AI-agent expertise)
Three Multi-MCP chain configurations: Slack+CodeExecutor, Playwright+Filesystem, Gmail+CodeExecutor+Filesystem
quantitative_results: Average ARR: T-MAP 57.8%, SE 32.5%, IR 15.6%, MT 10.0%, ZS 1.9% (Table 1)
Average RR: T-MAP 12.5%, SE 23.1%, IR 50.3%, MT 63.1%, ZS 87.8% (Table 1)
Per-environment T-MAP ARR/RR: CodeExecutor 56.2%/14.1%, Slack 64.1%/15.6%, Gmail 46.9%/9.4%, Playwright 37.5%/21.9%, Filesystem 84.4%/1.6%
Diversity (Table 2): |HL3| T-MAP 21.80 vs SE 12.80/IR 9.20/MT 6.00/ZS 1.20; Self-BLEU T-MAP 0.25 vs SE 0.45/IR 0.30/MT 0.32/ZS 0.33; cosine similarity T-MAP 0.47 vs 0.52 for all four baselines
Judge-human correlation (Table 3): Spearman/Pearson vs GPT-5.2 0.938/0.940, vs Opus 4.6 0.892/0.891, vs Qwen3.5-397B 0.969/0.968, vs Human 0.831/0.830
Human-judge confusion matrix: 91.2% agreement at L0, 70.7% at L1, 52.3% at L2, 65.3% at L3; 29.8% of human-L3 samples scored as L2 by the judge
Multi-MCP cross-server trajectory ratio (Table 5): T-MAP 46.28% vs ZS 14.29%, MT 20.55%, IR 22.97%, SE 16.42%
baselines_compared: Zero-Shot (ZS): one prompt per archive cell from risk category + attack style + tool definitions, no iteration
Multi-Trial (MT): repeated independent ZS sampling per iteration, no trajectory feedback
Iterative Refinement (IR): refines a prompt using its own trajectory and judge feedback within a single cell, following Zhou et al. 2025
Standard Evolution (SE): MAP-Elites mutation of elite parent prompts toward a target cell without trajectory analysis, following Samvelyan et al. 2024 (Rainbow Teaming)
